segments.s256-g65: ز + noun phrase marks source/starting point (poetic equivalent of از). گردان گزین is a genitive/attributive phrase “chosen/elite troop(s)”. کرد is the simple past of کردن used here causatively/constitutively (“made/formed/chose”), so the cluster reads “from the chosen troop(s) he made/selected…”.
segments.s256-g66: همه Rِ(زم‌جویان) is an inclusive modifier “all the warriors”; سازنده کار is a nominal phrase meaning “accomplishing/efficient in deed” with سازنده functioning adjectivally (‘constructive/doing’) and کار as the noun ‘deed/work’. Word order is noun modifiers after the head, typical of Persian poetic style.
segments.s256-g67: به introduces the indirect object or recipient (“to/for”); شیده is an adjective or epithet meaning “love‑struck/lovelorn.” که begins a relative clause “who…”, بودش is the enclitic past‑form of بودن (‘it was for him’ ≈ ‘he was’), and نبرده (negative perfect/past participle) indicates ‘had not borne/produced’ — here ‘had not sired’. پسر is the direct noun ‘son’. The clause therefore describes that person as one who had no son.
segments.s256-g68: ز marks source (“from”); گردان جنگی = ‘a fighting/military troop’ (گردان modified by جنگی). برآورده is a past participle/verb form from برآوردن meaning ‘raised up/produced’ (here ‘set up’/‘made leader of’), and سر is used metaphorically as ‘head/chief/leader’. The phrase thus says he raised up from that troop a warlike chief.
segments.s256-g69: بدو گفت is the common classical sequence ‘to him he said’ (بدو = به + او ‘to him’). گفت here is the speech verb (‘said’). کین لشکر سرفراز — in this position کین functions as a demonstrative/emphatic pointer (‘this/that’) in poetry and لشکر سرفراز is the object ‘victorious/proud army’; the whole gives the object of the speech.
segments.s256-g70: سپردم is the first‑person past of سپردن ‘to entrust/hand over’: “I entrusted”. The clitic ت is the 2nd‑person object suffix ‘you’. را is the definite/object marker attaching to the following noun phrase (here emphasizing what was entrusted). راه خوارزم ساز is an imperative infinitive construction: راه = ‘way/road’, خوارزم = destination, and ساز is the verb (make/prepare) governing the command ‘make/prepare the road to Khwarezm’ (in poetry the verb often appears as the bare stem).
segments.s256-g71: Imperative construction: نگهبان (predicate noun) + باش (imperative ‘be’) yields the command ‘Be guardian/protector’. آن مرز خوارزم is a determiner phrase ‘that border of Khwarezm’ (آن = that, مرز = border, خوارزم = Khwarezm) serving as the complement of نگهبان.
segments.s256-g72: This is a second imperative clause. همیشه = ‘always’ (adverbial), کمربسته is an adjectival/predicative participle ‘girded/girded about the waist’ (i.e., ready), رزم = ‘battle/war’ names the context, and باش is the imperative ‘be’. Together: ‘Always be girded (i.e., ready) for battle.’
segments.s256-g41: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. افراسیاب is the subject (proper name). آن سخنها is the definite object phrase 'those words' (آن + plural noun). شنود is an archaic/poetic third-person verb form meaning 'he heard' (past/simple narrative here). Word order is basically 'When Afrasiab those words heard'.
segments.s256-g42: برافروخت is a past narrative verb used figuratively ('he was kindled/was stirred' — here positive: 'elated'). از introduces the cause/reason ('because of' or 'by'); بخت here means 'fortune' or 'good luck'; و simply links the next noun; شادی 'joy' is the thing experienced; نمود is the past of نمودن 'to show/display' (here 'he displayed/showed'). Together: 'he was stirred by fortune and showed joy.'
segments.s256-g43: ابر here is a poetic epithet placed before پهلوانان; literally 'cloud' but used as an intensifier meaning 'great/peerless' champion. و links the next phrase. بر before موبدان functions like 'to/against/upon' marking the indirect target ('to the mobeds'). موبدان is the plural of mōbed (Zoroastrian priests).
segments.s256-g44: بکرد is the past narration 'he did/made'. آفرینی here is a noun/exclamation 'afarinī' meaning an acclamation or 'bravo!' (i.e. applause/cheer). به رسم is 'in the custom/rite of'; ردان is a poetic/historical term here (derived from a root meaning 'to drive') and is the name/class referenced by the custom phrase — the exact social meaning is specialized/poetic. The whole phrase means 'he gave a cheer according to the custom of the radan.'
segments.s256-g45: نویسنده is the agent 'the scribe/writer' (here functioning as the scribe). نامه را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker on 'the letter'. پیش here means 'in the presence of/before' (i.e. read aloud before the assembly or court). خواند is the past tense 'he read/recited.' The verb phrase order is object-marked noun + adverbial 'پیش' + verb.
segments.s256-g46: سخنهای is the plural 'words/speeches.' بایسته is an adjective 'fitting/necessary' modifying سخنها. چندی is an indefinite numeral/adverb 'some/a few.' براند is an older/poetic verb form related to راندن; here it means 'he put forth/uttered' (metaphorical extension 'to bring forth words'). The clause thus means 'he uttered a few fitting words.'
segments.s256-g47: فرستادگان is the subject plural 'the envoys/messengers.' خواست is the past-tense verb 'they requested/wished/asked.' از انجمن is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the assembly' marking the body to which the request was addressed ('they asked of the assembly').
segments.s256-g48: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to the presence of / near' indicating approach toward a person. فغفور is a title here (the Faghfur, i.e. the Byzantine emperor). و connects the two rulers. شاه is 'king.' ختن in this line is the proper name 'Khotan' (a place/kingdom), not the verbal lemma meaning 'to circumcise' — the provided lexical sense list does not fit this proper-name usage.
segments.s256-g49: فرستاد is a past tense verb (3rd person singular) 'sent'; the subject is omitted (implicit 'he/they'). نامه is the direct object placed after the verb. به هر کشوری is a prepositional phrase ('to/for every country') showing destination; کشوری is the adjectival/relational form of کشور used as a noun.
segments.s256-g50: The preposition به begins a coordinated list of targets. هر names distributive 'every/everyone' and نامداری and مهتری are coordinated by و; each noun refers to a class of persons (notables, chiefs). Word order is preposition + determiner + noun, parallel to the previous phrase.
segments.s256-g51: سپه is the subject 'the army'. خواست is past 'wanted/desired'. کاندیشه جنگ داشت is a subordinate clause: کاندیشه ('the thought/intention') governed by جنگ ('of war'), and داشت is a possession-type verb 'had' (i.e. 'it held the thought/intent'). The sequence therefore states desire followed by possession of the intention to fight.
segments.s256-g52: ز introduces cause/reason here ('because of' / 'on account of') followed by the name بیژن. the sequence ب + دان = بدان forms the adverbial phrase 'بدان گونه' meaning 'in that manner' or 'so'; گونه is 'manner'. دل‌تنگ داشت uses داشت ('had') with دل‌تنگ ('sorrowful / homesick') — a possession/experiential construction meaning 'felt sorrow/longing.'
segments.s256-g53: دو هفته is a temporal expression 'two weeks' placed before the verb for emphasis. برآمد is past 'arose / came forth / issued forth'. ز چین و ختن is the source phrase 'from China and Khotan' (ختن here a place name). The construction expresses that, after two weeks, forces came from those lands.
segments.s256-g54: ز هر کشوری 'from every country' marks origin/participants. شد is the past of شدن 'became / came to be' and here introduces the result: سپاه انجمن is a noun phrase 'the army of the assembly' (noun + governing noun), i.e. the assembled host. Word order is idiomatic Persian: source phrase → شد → result noun phrase.
segments.s256-g55: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when/as'. دریای جوشان is a nominal phrase 'the boiling/boiling-up sea' with adjective after noun (poetic order). زمین is the noun 'the earth/ground' and بردمید is a past verb 'lifted up / carried off / rose' describing a violent, sweeping motion — poetic imagery where the earth behaves like a boiling sea.
segments.s256-g56: چنان شد is a fixed resultative 'it became so' (چنان = 'so/such', شد = 'became'). که introduces the resulting clause. کس روز روشن ندید literally 'no one saw the bright day' — ندید is past negated 'did not see', and with کس here carries the sense 'no one', yielding 'no one saw daylight.'
segments.s256-g33: كِه introduces a subordinate content clause ('that'). The structure ما را ... بباید گذشت is an older/poetic impersonal/modal pattern meaning 'it is necessary that we cross / we must cross'; ما را carries an accusative/topic marking on the person affected. بباید is the modal 'must/ought', and گذشت here functions as the verbal notion 'to pass / cross' (infinitival/nominalized sense) governed by the modal.
segments.s256-g34: This is an infinitival noun phrase headed by the verb زدن 'to strike/sound' used with a musical/instrumental meaning; کوس شاهی is a genitive/attributive NP 'the royal war-drum'. بر آن پهن دشت is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'upon that broad plain' (بر + demonstrative + adjective + noun). The whole phrase functions as the action accompanying or intended after crossing the river.
segments.s256-g35: به introduces the goal/place 'at/to'; آموی here refers to the Amu (river) with an old genitive/locative form, and لشکرگهی ('لشکرگه' + adjectival/nominalizing -ی) means 'army‑camp'. ساختن is the infinitive 'to make/establish'. Together this is an infinitival locative clause 'to make an army-camp at the Amu.'
segments.s256-g36: شب و روز is the adverbial phrase 'day and night'. ناسودن is an archaic/poetic verb meaning here 'not to cease / be unprofitable to stop' (i.e. be unceasing); it is followed by از + تاختن, where از marks the activity from which one does not desist and تاختن is the action 'to charge/raid'. The whole means 'to be unceasing in charging day and night.'
segments.s256-g37: که introduces explanation/causal content again. آن جای جنگست is a nominal predicate with the copula -ست ('is') meaning 'that is the place of war/battle'. و خون ریختن places an infinitival noun phrase 'and (of) shedding blood' in apposition — 'the place of battle and of blood‑shedding.'
segments.s256-g38: چه here functions as a focusing/illustrative particle ('namely / that is'), introducing the specific activity: با گیو و با رستم آویختن is an infinitival clause 'to grapple/engage with Giv and with Rostam'. آویختن is a present participial/infinitival verb meaning 'to cling to / grapple with' in the combat sense; the repeated با marks the two opponents linked by و.
segments.s256-g39: سرافراز is an epithet 'proud / triumphant' placed before گردان 'troop'. گیرنده is a present‑participle adjective 'seizing / taking' that modifies گردان, and شهر is its object 'city'. Together: 'the proud, city‑capturing troop.'
segments.s256-g40: همه تیغ کین is a noun chain 'all the swords of vengeance' (همه = 'all/every', تیغ = 'sword', کین specifying their vengeful character). آب‌داده is a participial adjective 'watered/drenched' and به زهر marks the medium 'with/in poison' (به marking the instrument/medium). The phrase describes the troop's blades as steeped in poisonous, vengeful blood — a poetic depiction.
segments.s256-g25: سزد‌گر is an impersonal/optative particle meaning 'if it befit' or 'if one deems it fitting' and stands at the clause head; کنون is an adverb 'now'; گرد این کشورم is a prepositional/locative phrase 'around this realm' with گرد functioning as 'around/about' before the noun and کشورم carrying the 1st‑person possessive suffix '-م' ('my realm').
segments.s256-g26: سراسر is an adverbial 'throughout' modifying فرستادگان (messengers); فرستادگان is the direct object 'messengers, envoys'; گسترم is a 1st‑person verb form ('-م' = I) from گستر(یدن) meaning 'I spread / I dispatch', the clause’s verbal head completing the sentence.
segments.s256-g27: ز ... واز is a coordinate pair of prepositional phrases 'from the Turks and from China' (واز = و + از 'and from'); هزاران هزار is a reduplicative poetic expression 'thousands and thousands' = 'myriad, countless', following the source phrases.
segments.s256-g28: کمربستگان is a plural participial noun 'those who have girded (their loins)' i.e. 'warriors prepared for battle'; از در کارزار is a locative/idiomatic phrase where در here means 'gate/threshold' and کارزار 'battle/field of battle' — together conveying 'at the gate/field of battle' or 'ready for the fight'.
segments.s256-g29: بیاریم is a 1st‑person plural optative/imperative 'let us bring' or 'we will bring' (classical usage); بر گرد ایران is the locative phrase 'round/about Iran' (بر + گرد used to mark circumambience); سپاه is the object 'an army, host'.
segments.s256-g30: بسازیم is 1st‑person plural 'let us build/establish'; هر سو is 'every direction/side'; یکی Rِزمگاه is 'one (a single) battlefield' — the phrase as a whole instructs to establish (a) battle‑site on each side/direction.
segments.s256-g31: همه موبدان = 'all the mobeds' (mobed = Zoroastrian priests); رای هشیار = 'prudent/sensible counsel' (رای = opinion/counsel; هشیار qualifies it as prudent/sane); خوی + ش together form the possessive 'خویش' meaning 'their own' (here 'their counsel').
segments.s256-g32: نهادند is the past tense verb 'they placed/put down/established' (here 'they offered/adopted'); با takes an accompaniment/instrument sense 'with/by'; گفت here nominally means 'word/saying' (i.e. 'the word/speech/consent'); سالار خویش = 'their commander/chief' (سالار = commander, خوی + ش = their). The whole phrase means they adopted their prudent counsel in accordance with / together with their leader's word.
segments.s256-g17: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/since' (like modern از) giving a temporal sense. هنگام رزم is a genitive phrase 'at the time of battle; since the fighting' with هنگام preceding the noun رزم. منوچهر is the proper name that specifies whose fighting is meant. باز is an adverb 'again' placed before the verb it modifies.
segments.s256-g18: نبد is the archaic/poetic negative of بودن (نبود), here 'was not / did not exist'. دست ایران is the subject phrase meaning 'the hand/power of Iran' (دست used metonymically as power/control). به introduces the direction 'toward/into' and توران is the target. دراز is an adjective 'long/extended' describing the reach — together 'دست ... دراز' = 'the power did not extend (to)'.
segments.s256-g19: شبیخون کند is a present/simple tense clause 'make a night-raid'; کند is the verb 'do/make' (here generic/indefinite subject). تا introduces a purpose clause ('so that / in order that'), which governs the following verb in the subjunctive. در خان من = 'into my khan/inn/house' — Persian places the possessor (من) after the noun.
segments.s256-g20: از ایران بیازند is the content of the purpose clause: از ایران 'from/out of Iran' and بیازند is the plural present/subjunctive of the verb یازخت/یاخت in the sense 'break forth, burst in'. بر جان من means 'upon/against my life' (بر here 'on/upon'), i.e. they intend to attack the speaker’s life; من again functions as the possessor 'my'.
segments.s256-g21: دلاور شد is a past action 'became brave/valiant' with شد as the past-tense copula/verb 'became'. آن مردم is the demonstrative + noun 'that people'; نادلیر is a postpositive adjective ('not-lionhearted' = cowardly) describing those people. Word order places the predicate first for emphasis ('valiant became that cowardly people').
segments.s256-g22: گوزن is the subject 'a deer/stag'. اندر آمد = 'entered/came in' where اندر is a prepositional/adverbial 'in/within' and آمد is the past 'came'. به بالین شیر = 'to/by the side of the lion': به marks direction/approach and بالین here means 'beside; at the side of' (not 'pillow'), with شیر as the noun possessed/related.
segments.s256-g23: بر این کینه‌گر is a prepositional phrase 'against/onto this vindictive person' (بر here 'against'). کار سازیم is first-person plural سازیم 'we will/do make/arrange the matter' — in epic Persian present can carry a near-future sense, so 'we will deal with it.' زود is an adverb 'soon/quickly' modifying سازیم.
segments.s256-g24: وگرنه 'otherwise' introduces the adverse consequence. برآرند is a plural present/subjunctive form meaning 'they bring forth/produce/raise' (here 'raise' or 'bring upon'). زین مرز دود in classical/poetic usage reads as زِ این (a contracted/poetic 'from/of this') + مرز دود 'border smoke' — i.e. 'they will raise smoke on/from this frontier' (threat of burning/raiding). Note that زین is an archaic/poetic contraction (ز + این).
segments.s256-g73: دگر modifies پنجه and means 'another' or 'a further'; پنجه here is a poetic collective meaning 'a group of five' (a quintet). از introduces the source 'from'; نامداران چین is a genitive phrase 'the renowned (men) of Chin/China' with the head noun نامداران followed by the place-name چین.
segments.s256-g74: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning 'he commanded/ordered/said' (courtly past). تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that / in order that'). کرد is a causative/past verb 'made/caused' (here functioning with the following verb); پیران is the object 'the elders'; گزین is the verb 'to choose' (infinitive/subjunctival idea). Together this clause means he ordered that the elders be chosen.
segments.s256-g75: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو is the dative 'to him/unto him' placed before گفت). تا again marks purpose/command 'so that / in order that'. شهر ایران is the locative destination 'the city of Iran' (noun + place name), and برو is the imperative 'go' placed at the end as is usual in Persian.
segments.s256-g76: ممان is a negative imperative/formal admonition meaning 'do not withhold / do not delay' and governs the following noun رخت which here means 'departure' (an archaic meaning of رخت). و links the two phrases. مه is a poetic epithet here qualifying تخت (literally 'moon' or 'glory' used poetically); تخت سالار نو = 'the throne of the new lord' (تخت = throne, سالار = lord/commander, نو = new). The whole phrase urges prompt departure to the new sovereign's throne.
segments.s256-g77: در آشتی ('in reconciliation') is a prepositional/locative phrase; هیچ‌گونه is an emphatic negative quantifier 'not in any way / no kind whatsoever' placed before the verb; مجوی is the negative imperative/subjunctive 'do not seek' (2nd person directive). The clause forbids any attempt at reconciliation.
segments.s256-g78: سخن is the noun 'speech/words' (direct object). جز is the restrictive preposition 'except'. به جنگ و به کینه are prepositional phrases 'of/for war and of/for hatred' specifying the only permitted subject matter. مگوی is the negative imperative 'do not say' (2nd person). The order places the verb at the end, typical of Persian.
segments.s256-g79: کسی کو is an archaic relative/indefinite 'anyone who / he who' (کو used in place of که). برد here functions as a past verb 'carried / brought' (he who has brought/taken). آب و آتش بهم = 'water and fire together' with بهم indicating 'together' or 'into combination.' The clause describes the (impossible/unnatural) joining of opposites.
segments.s256-g80: ابر is the grammatical subject 'the cloud' (or 'a cloud/force' used metaphorically). هر دوان = 'who/which has driven/rushed both' (هر = each/any; دوان = caused to run/hasten). کرده باشد is a perfect subjunctive or modal perfect 'has done / may have done' used here to indicate completed action. ستم is the predicate noun 'injustice/oppression' — i.e. the cloud (so described) has committed wrongdoing.
segments.s256-g57: گله هرچ بودش ز اسبان یله is a compact nominal phrase: گله (herd) is the head, modified by هرچ (literally “whatever/however many”) to mean “whichever herd(s) there were.” بودش is an archaic past copula/enclitic (بود + ـش) meaning “there was/he had,” placed after هرچ as in classical Persian. ز is the poetic preposition ‘of/from’ linking گله to the following noun phrase اسبان یله (horses that are yelah); اسبان (horses) + یله (an adjective ‘loose/unrestrained’) together mean “loose horses.”
segments.s256-g58: به شهر اندر آورد یکسر گله is the verb phrase: به شهر اندر آورد is an old-fashioned way to say “brought into the city” (به/اندر = to/into + آورد = brought). Classical Persian often places locatives before the verb. یکسر modifies گله and means “entire/whole,” so یکسر گله is the definite object “the whole herd”; the verb آورد precedes that object here, producing the literal order “brought into the city the whole herd.”
segments.s256-g59: همان گنجها کز گه تور باز begins the relative clause: همان (that/the very) identifies the treasures (گنجها). کز is the relative particle “which/that (from)” and گه here = “time/occasion,” so کز گه تور باز = “which from the time Tur returned/again” (تور is the proper name Tūr and باز = ‘again/returned’). The phrase sets the temporal frame for the relative clause that follows.
segments.s256-g60: پدر بر پسربر همی داشت راز is the remainder of the relative clause: پدر = “the father.” بر is the classical preposition meaning “toward/on/against” here functioning like modern به/از depending on idiom; پسربر appears as a compound/epithet (likely a name or title rather than literal “son-carrier”), so بر پسربر = “toward/with respect to Pesarbar.” همی is the classical continuous/habitual marker “would/used to,” and داشت راز = “kept the secret.” Together: “the father would keep the secret from/withheld it regarding Pesarbar.”
segments.s256-g61: سر بدره‌ها را گشادن گرفت is a classical verb cluster: سر بدره‌ها (literally “the heads/faces of the badrehā”) — بدره here is a poetic word for a beautiful woman or ‘full-moon’ beauty — and را marks it as the definite object. گشادن (to open/reveal/uncover) is the lexical verb and گرفت (literally “took”) is used as a light verb auxiliary meaning “began/commenced” in this construction: “took to uncovering” = “began to unveil.”
segments.s256-g62: شب و روز دینار دادن گرفت is parallel: شب و روز (“night and day”) is an adverbial phrase, دینار is the coin (object), دادن is the infinitive ‘to give’ and گرفت again functions as the light-verb ‘began/took to.’ The construction دینار دادن گرفت therefore means “he began to give dinars” (i.e. he started lavish payments).
segments.s256-g63: چو لشکر سراسر شد آراسته is a temporal clause: چو = “when,” لشکر = “army/host,” سراسر شد = “became entire/complete” (سراسر as predicate with شد ‘became’), and آراسته is a past-participial/adjectival complement “adorned/arrayed.” The copular structure places the adjective after شد in classical style: “when the army had become entirely arrayed/prepared.”
segments.s256-g64: بدان بی‌نیازی شد از خواسته contains a classical adverbial/causal phrase and result: بدان (a fused form equivalent to با آن / ‘with that’ or ‘by that’) introduces the consequence; بی‌نیازی شد = “became without-need” (became self‑sufficient/indifferent to want), and از خواسته = “from desire/want” specifying what he was free of. Together: “with that he became self‑sufficient and free from desire.” Note: دان here is part of the fused prepositional phrase بدان (not the verb ‘to know’), so its lexical senses listed above do not match this usage.
segments.s256-g81: پر + مایه here form the adjective پرمایه (written as two words for the poem’s meter) meaning “full of substance/able/resourceful.” The adjective بیدار (postpositive after the compound) qualifies those two people; the conjunction و links that phrase to the following noun دو پهلوان.
segments.s256-g82: یکی ... و دیگر ... enumerates the two champions: یکی introduces the first (here described as پیر و باهوش), و links the two properties, and دیگر picks out the second (جوان). پیر is the ordinary adjective “old,” باهوش “intelligent/clever,” and دیگر here means “the other/another.” Word order is typical Persian: determiner/number + adjective(s) + (implied noun) or apposition.
segments.s256-g83: برفتند is the past plural verb “they went/left.” با + پند is the prepositional phrase “with (the) advice,” and افراسیاب is the proper name in the genitive role as the source of the advice. Word order is verb + adjunct (typical narrative past).
segments.s256-g84: This line contrasts the two persons’ manners: به آرام is an adverbial phrase meaning “calmly/slowly,” applying to پیر, while بر شتاب is a parallel adverbial “with haste” applying to جوان. The preposition به here forms an adverbial, and بر + شتاب is another adverbial construction; the coordinating و links پیر and جوان.
segments.s256-g85: This is an itemized instrumental phrase: the initial token (ابا) is archaic/poetic here and functions like a marker introducing the items (see note). ترگ زرین and کوپال and تیغ enumerate arms or accoutrements; زرین is the adjective “golden” modifying ترگ, کوپال is a named weapon (or name of a weapon), and تیغ is “sword/blade.” Word order places modifiers after nouns in apposition due to poetic phrasing.
segments.s256-g86: خروشان is an adjective/participle “roaring/tumultuous” qualifying تیغ; به کردار is a prepositional phrase meaning “in the manner of,” where به takes an adverbial sense, کردار = “conduct/way,” and غرنده میغ is a simile-like phrase “snarling/roaring blade” (غرنده modifies میغ). Together the phrase compares the weapon’s behavior to a roaring blade.
segments.s256-g1: دل شاه ترکان is a genitive phrase (the heart of the Turk king) with head noun دل followed by its possessors in the order 'شاه ترکان' (king of the Turks). چنان کم شنود is a classical-past/simple-stative comment: شنود is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person present/past-form meaning 'hears/was inclined to hear' and کم here functions adverbially ('scarcely/rarely'), preceded by چنان ('so'), giving 'so rarely listens'. The phrase as a whole says whose heart it is and qualities of that heart.
segments.s256-g2: همیشه begins the clause as an adverb 'always'. به رنج is a prepositional phrase 'into/with suffering' (به used as 'towards/into'). از پی آز is a compact expression: از + پی (here 'in pursuit of') + آز ('greed'), meaning 'from/in pursuit of greed'. بود is the past copula 'was', so the clause states a continual state: 'he was always in torment, following greed.'
segments.s256-g3: از آن پس که is a temporal subordinating string 'from that time/after that when'. برگشت is the verb 'he returned' (simple past). زان رزمگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from that battlefield' (زان = 'from that', رزمگاه = 'battlefield'), completing the temporal clause: 'from the time he returned from that battlefield'.
segments.s256-g4: که introduces a relative/time clause 'when/that'. رستم is the subject name. بر او here uses the preposition بر ('upon/against') plus pronoun او ('him'), marking the target of رستم's action. کرد is the past tense verb 'did/made' and گیتی سیاه is the object-complement 'the world [turned] dark' (گیتی 'world', سیاه 'dark/gloomy'). The line means Rostam made the world dark for him; word order is Subject – oblique target – verb – nominal result.
segments.s256-g5: بشد is an archaic/poetic verb-form meaning 'it happened / they arose / it became' (here narrating that the Arabs moved). تازیان is the subject 'the Arabs'. تا introduces a limit 'until'. به + خلخ indicates motion toward/arrival 'to Khalkh' (خلخ here a place-name/contextual target). رسید is the past verb 'reached'. The clause therefore narrates the Arabs' advance up to that point.
segments.s256-g6: به ننگ is literally 'to/with shame' marking the manner or cause; از کیان 'from/of the Kayanids' (the royal house) gives whose shame it was. شد is the past copula 'became' and سرش ناپدید is a nominal phrase 'his head vanished' (سر + enclitic ش 'his' + ناپدید 'vanished'). The sequence expresses that, in disgrace before the Kayan kings, his head disappeared (i.e., he was ruined/vanished).
segments.s256-g7: به کاخ اندر آمد is literal motion 'entered into the palace' (به + place + اندر + آمد). پر آزار is an adjectival phrase 'full of distress/torment' (پر 'full (of)', آزار 'distress/annoyance') modifying دل 'the heart', so the whole segment describes his entry with the inner state 'his heart full of anguish'.
segments.s256-g8: ابا is an older/poetic verb/noun meaning 'to refuse / to be reluctant' and here takes کاردانان هشیاردل as its object: کاردانان is the plural noun 'the skilled/experienced ones' and هشیاردل is a compound adjective 'clear‑/alert‑hearted' (i.e., sensible, watchful). Together this short clause says he was unwilling/reluctant with respect to the capable, clear‑hearted advisers.
segments.s256-g9: چو introduces a comparison/listing meaning “like” or “as,” followed by the names/agents (پیران و گرسیوز). رهنمون (= “guide, guide‑role”) is a nominal predicate/appositive that characterises those names. Word order is comparatival marker + noun phrases + appositive; there is no overt verb here.
segments.s256-g10: This is a continued list: قراخان (a titled name) joined by و, then چون used again as “like/as” followed by شیده and another name/word گرسیون. The structure groups names and likenesses; چون repeats the comparative sense rather than signalling a temporal clause.
segments.s256-g11: بر ایشان here means “to/for them” (the preposition بر with the pronoun ایشان); همه داستان is the definite object “the whole story/ tale,” and برگشاد is a past participle/verb meaning “opened” or “unfolded.” The clause literally reads “(the) whole tale was opened to them” (agent implied or carried by the context).
segments.s256-g12: This clause puts the temporal/qualifying phrase گذشته سخنها (“past words/accounts”) before the verb complex همه کرد یاد. کرد + یاد functions together as a light-verb construction meaning “made remembrance” = “recalled/remembered.” Word order is object/prepositional phrase → auxiliary verb → nominal complement.
segments.s256-g13: که تا marks purpose/temporal sense “so that/when” (classical usage where که + تا expresses the aim or occasion). برنهادم is the present/subjunctive/past‑relative form of برنهاد “to place on,” and به شاهی کلاه literally “the hat/crown upon kingship” (کلاه = hat/crown, شاهی = kingship). The clause expresses the purpose/act of placing the royal crown.
segments.s256-g14: مرا here is a beneficiary/topic marker “for me,” followed by the past verb گشت “became.” The grammatical subject (خورشید و تابنده ماه) follows the verb, a common classical Persian order (verb before subject). The clause means “the sun and the shining moon became (for) me.”
segments.s256-g15: مرا بود is a copular construction where مرا (= “for me”) marks the beneficiary/topic and بود is the past copula “was/had.” The predicate بر مهتران دسترس means “access to the chiefs/nobles” (بر = to/toward + مهتران = chiefs; دسترس = reach/access). Word order places the beneficiary and copula before the predicate phrase.
segments.s256-g16: This clause is negative in force by context: عنان مرا (my reins/bridle for me) + برنتابید (past form meaning “could not hold/tolerate/restrain” in this usage) + کس (no one/anyone). The order places the object/topic (مرا) before the verb; the whole means “no one could restrain my reins” (i.e. none could control me).

sentences.s256-s33: From the chosen ranks he made five thousand, all battle‑waging men who accomplish deeds.
sentences.s256-s34: To the lovelorn one — who had not yet begotten a son — he raised up from the fighting ranks a warlike leader.
sentences.s256-s35: He said to him: “I have entrusted to you this proud/victorious army; make the way to Khwarezm.”
sentences.s256-s36: Be the guardian of that Khwarezm border; always gird your loins and be ready for battle.
sentences.s256-s21: When Afrasiab heard those words, he was elated by fortune and showed joy.
sentences.s256-s22: He cried 'bravo' to the foremost champions and to the mobeds, performing the acclamation in the manner of the radan.
sentences.s256-s23: The scribe read the letter aloud before them and uttered a few appropriate words.
sentences.s256-s24: The envoys requested of the assembly to approach the Faghfur and the king of Khotan.
sentences.s256-s25: He sent letters to every country — to every notable and every chief.
sentences.s256-s26: The army desired war — it entertained the thought of battle; and because of Bijan it felt such longing.
sentences.s256-s27: After two weeks forces rose up from China and Khotan; from every country the assembly's army was formed.
sentences.s256-s28: When the earth rose up like a boiling sea, it became such that no one saw the daylight.
sentences.s256-s17: that we must cross the Jeyhun and sound the royal drum upon that wide plain.
sentences.s256-s18: to make a camp at the Amu and, day and night, be unceasing in charging.
sentences.s256-s19: for that is the place of battle and of bloodshed — namely, to grapple with Giv and with Rostam.
sentences.s256-s20: the proud troop that takes the city — all their swords, instruments of vengeance, drenched in poison.
sentences.s256-s13: If it befit now, I will send messengers throughout this realm.
sentences.s256-s14: From the Turks and from China, thousands upon thousands (came), girded for battle.
sentences.s256-s15: Let us bring an army around Iran; let us make a battlefield in every direction.
sentences.s256-s16: All the mobeds set down their prudent counsel, with the words of their chief.
sentences.s256-s9: Since the time of Manūchehr’s battles, Iran’s power did not again reach out to Turan.
sentences.s256-s10: They make a night-raid so that they may burst into my khan from Iran against my life.
sentences.s256-s11: That people, who had been cowardly, became valiant; a stag came in beside the lion.
sentences.s256-s12: We will deal with this vindictive one quickly; otherwise they will raise smoke upon/from this frontier.
sentences.s256-s37: He dispatched another quintet of China's renowned men and commanded them to select elders.
sentences.s256-s38: He said to him: 'Go to the city of Iran; do not delay your departure, and (go to) the throne of the new lord.'
sentences.s256-s39: Seek no kind of reconciliation; speak no words except those of war and of hatred.
sentences.s256-s40: Whoever has joined water and fire together — any cloud that has rushed them both has committed injustice.
sentences.s256-s29: Whatever herds of loose horses he had, he brought the whole herd into the city.
sentences.s256-s30: Those same treasures which, from the time when Tur returned, the father had kept secret from the (young) Pesarbar.
sentences.s256-s31: He began to unveil the faces of the beauties, and night and day he began giving dinars.
sentences.s256-s32: When the army was wholly prepared and arrayed, by that he became self-sufficient and free from desire.
sentences.s256-s41: Two resourceful, alert men and two champions — one old and clever and the other young.
sentences.s256-s42: They departed with Afrasiab’s counsel; the elder went calmly and the young with haste.
sentences.s256-s43: With a golden terg and Kupāl and a roaring sword, like a snarling blade.
sentences.s256-s1: The heart of the Turkish king so seldom listened; he was always in torment, pursuing greed.
sentences.s256-s2: Since he returned from that battlefield — when Rostam made the world dark for him.
sentences.s256-s3: The Arabs rose up and advanced until they reached Khalkh; because of shame before the Kayanids, his head became vanished.
sentences.s256-s4: He entered the palace, his heart full of torment; reluctant toward the skilled, clear‑hearted counselors.
sentences.s256-s5: As guides, Piran and Garsivaz — Qara Khan — and likewise Shideh and Garsiyon.
sentences.s256-s6: He opened the whole tale to them and called to mind all the past words.
sentences.s256-s7: So that, when I set the crown upon kingship, the sun and the shining moon became mine.
sentences.s256-s8: I had access to the chiefs; no one could restrain my reins.
